array.string: God promises to raise up a seed that would break the works of Satan (Gen 3:15)
Jehovah declares man’s days will amount to 120 years (deluge after)
Noach and family build the ark
Jehovah states that He will make it rain in seven days (Gen 7:4).
Ark comes to rest on mountains of Ararat
Noah removes part of the roof
Noah and family go out of the ark
Birth of Isaac as heir of the covenant Genesis 17:19 and 21:1-5)
400-year affliction upon Abraham’s offspring (Isaac was about 5 and Ishmael about 19)
Eliezer is sent to find a wife for Isaac
Jacob flees to Haran (away from Esau)
Jacob dreams of a stairway to heaven
Jacob returns to Canaan from Haran
Jehovah confirms that the seed will come via Jacob, the son of Isaac (Genesis 28:10-15)
Jacob wrestles angel and is named Israel
Potiphar’s wife tries to seduce Joseph
The cupbearer and the baker explain their dreams to Joseph
Joseph is released and is made second ruler of Egypt, after Pharaoh
Joseph gets 2 sons: Manasseh and Ephraim
The brothers of Joseph come to Egypt to buy food
Jacob and his family arrive in Egypt
Jacob acknowledges that the seed will come via his son Juda (Genesis 49:10)
Moses kills Egyptian and flees to Midian
Arrival in the wilderness of Sinai (Ex. 19:1)
Law covenant in the third month after Exodus from Egypt (Ex 19:1)
Sanctification ceremony. Installation of the priests
Moses registers the tribes and families (Num 1:1,2)
The cloud lifts from over the tabernacle for the first time as guide for the Israelites (Num 10:11)
2 spies arrive at Rahabs house in Jericho
Israelites celebrate the Passover in the desert plains of Jericho
The Israelites eat from the harvest of the Promised Land for the first time. Manna stops.
Major conquest of the Promised Land completed. End of the ‘about 450 years’ of Acts 13:17-20.
Israelites subject to 8 years of service to Mesopotamia
Israelites subject to 18 years of service to Moab (Eglon)
Israelites subject to 20 years of service to Canaanites (Jabin/Sisera)
Israelites subject to 7 years of service to Midian
Israelites subject to 18 years of service to Philistines/Ammonites
Israelites subject to 40 years of service to Philistines
Nation Israel has its first king (Saul)
Saul defeats the Ammonites with an army of 330,000 men
Saul preemptuously brings burnt offering because Samuel was later than expected (1 Sam 13:13)
David becomes king (first of Judah, after over all Israel)
David becomes king over all Israel. Jerusalem capital
King David learns that the seed will be a descendant from him and will reign as King for ever (2 Samuel 7:13-16 and Psalms 89:35, 36)
Israel divided into two kingdoms, Israel and Judah
Pharaoh Shishak invades Judah and takes treasures from temple
Peace during the reign of Asa
Asa removes idols, restores Jehovah’s altar, and unites the nation (2 Kron 15:10)
Asa calls Syria for help (as opposed to asking Jehovah) against Baasha of Israel.
Zimri becomes king of Israel (for 7 days. Unknown which 7 days exactly)
Asa gets an ailment in his feet
Jehoshaphat sends his princes to teach in the cities of Judah
Jehoshaphat fights together with Ahab against Ramoth-Gilead
Jehu executes King Jehoram of Israel and his brothers, Jehoram’s mother (Jezebel), and King Ahaziah of Judah and his brothers
Athaliah murders all the royal offspring and usurps the throne. Only her grandson Jehoash is saved and kept hidden from her by High Priest Jehoiada.
Queen Athaliah is executed by High Priest Jehoiada

Married by the time he was 21, he had two wives, one of whom was named Jehoaddan, and by these Jehoash became father to sons and daughters. In this way the line of David leading to the Messiah, which had come so near to being completely severed, was once again made strong.
King Je·hoʹash called Je·hoiʹa·da the priest and the other priests and said to them: Why are you not repairing the damage to the house?’
Jeroboam II becomes king of Israel
Shallum becomes king of Israel (for 1 month. Unknown which month exactly)
Tiglath-pileser of Assyria exiles portions of Israel
Hezekiah opened the doors of the temple and repaired them
Sennacherib attacks Judah, but 185,000 soldiers are killed in 1 night
Josiah started to cleanse Judah and Jerusalem of the high places and the sacred poles, the graven images, and the metal statues
Josiah commands to return the ark of the covenant to the temple
Josiah celebrates the Passover (it has been a long time)
Jehoahaz becomes king of Judah (3 months in the early part of the year 628 B.C.E., exact months unknown)
Jeremiah rebukes Baruch ‘You are seeking great things for yourself’
Baruch reads scroll in temple courtyard
Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem a second time, to punish the rebellious Jehoiakim (Jehoiakim likely dies)
Daniel and other ‘foremost men of the land’ are send as prisoners to Babylon
Jehoiachin surrenders after having reigned 3 months and 10 days
Ezekiel’s vision of false worship in temple
Nebuchadnezzar invades Judah for a 3rd time. Final siege of Jerusalem begins
Ezekiel record the day that Babylon attacks Jerusalem and relates a proverb concerning the cooking pot
Nineveh falls to Chaldeans and Medes
Baruch took the deeds for the property of Anathoth and put them in a vessel
Ezekiel prophesies that Babylon will fight Egypt and win
Ezekiel prophesies against Egypt, that it will be cut down as a tree
The desolation of Jerusalem is complete, Israelites’ exile in Babylon begins
Ezekiel sings a dirge concerning Egypt
Daniel is notified that the city has been struck down
Nebuchadnezzar exiles more Jews, conquers Moab and Ammon, and invades Egypt
Jehovah instructs Ezekiel to divide the Promised Land again (Ez 45:1)
Ezekiel prophesies that Babylon will receive Egypt as compensation
Cyrus the Great becomes king of Persia
Babylon falls on the night of 5 to 6 October
About 50,000 Jewish exiles return from Babylon under the leadership of Zerubbabel (or Sheshbazzar)
The Jews that returned to the Promised Land offered their first sacrifice
Foundation of temple in Jerusalem laid by Zerubbabel
Persian king Cyrus dies, Cambyses alias Ahasuerus replaces him
Cambyses or Ahasuerus - king of Persia
Artaxerxes or Bardiya or Gaumata - king of Persia
Xerxes I or Ahasuerus - king of Persia
Vashti refuses to appear at the banquet of Ahasuerus
Esther becomes queen and wife of Ahasuerus
Haman sends couriers with the plan to commit genocide to the Jews on Adar 13
Mordecai sends couriers with an updated plan for Adar 13
13 adar: the Jews exteminate their enemies
14 adar: the Jews exterminate the remaining enemies
15 adar: the Jews have peace and set the celebration of Poerim
Xerxes I of Persia moves a force of 2,317,610 men against Greece
Ezra travels from Babylon to Jerusalem with the Jewish remnant
Ezra and the group of Jews arrive in Jerusalem
The issue with the foreign women made public, but postponed. Reason: the men were ‘shivering because of the matter and because of the heavy rain’
Official start of the investigation about the foreign women
Matter about the foreign women settled
During the 20th year of King Artaxerxes, in the month Chislev (November-December), Nehemiah, while in Shushan the castle, received visitors, his brother Hanani and other men from Judah.
If it pleases the king..., send me to Judah’ (Nehemiah 2:5)
Issuing of the word to restore and rebuild Jerusalem
Jerusalem’s walls rebuilt in 52 days (by Nehemiah)
The returned Jews gather at the public square to listen to the reading of the Law (Neh. 8:1)
They learn that they need to celebrate the Festival of Booths (Neh 8:14)
At Tisjri 24 the Jews publicly show repentance (Neh 9:1)
Rebuilding of Jerusalem is evidently completed
Alexander the Great is born at Pella
Alexander the Great went back to Pella to rule Macedonia in the absence of his father
20-year-old Alexander the Great inherits the throne
Island Tyre destroyed by Alexander the Great
Ptolemy I Lagus assumed the title of king. Ptolemaic dynasty.
Alexander’s empire divided into 4 Hellenistic kingdoms: Cassander, Lysimachus, Seleucus I Nicator and Ptolemy I Lagus.
Lysimachus takes possession of Cassander’s part (Cassander died)
Seleucus I Nicator takes control over Lysimachus’ part. Seleucid dynasty
Antiochus II (Seleucid dynasty) marries Berenice (daughter of Ptolemy II - Ptolemaic dynasty)
Seleucus II Callinicus (Seleucid dynasty), son of Laodice
Judah under the domination of the Syrian king of the north
Antiochus IV Epiphane sacrificie a pig on the altaar van de tempel in Jeruzalem.
Rededication of temple after desecration by Greek idolatry (Festival of Dedication)
Herod (appointed king by Rome) takes Jerusalem
Angel Gabriel foretells the birth of John the Baptist to Zechariah
Angel Gabriel foretells the birth of Jesus to Mary
Presented in temple by parents (after 40th day)
Jozeph, Maria and Jezus return from Egypte
Caesar Augustus adopted Tiberius and made him heir to the throne
Twelve-year-old Jesus at the temple questions teachers
Paul studies in the school of Gamaliel
Pilate appointed as governor of Judea
John the Baptist begins his ministry
Jezus identified as the ‘seed’, the Messiah, the future judge, and appointed King (John 1:17 and 4:25, 26 and Acts 10:42, 43 and 2 Cor 1:20 and 1 Tim 3:16)
Disciples Andrew and John and Peter and James follow Jesus
Disciples Philip and Nathanael follow Jesus
Jesus calls 4 disciples: Simon and Andrew, James and John
First tour of Galilee (with the four)
Jesus reveals that he will have fellow rulers and judges, that the heavenly Kingdom will have earthly subjects, and that all his followers are to be Kingdom preachers
Lucius Sejanus (friend of Pontius Pilate) amidst suspicions of conspiracy against Tiberius, was arrested and executed
Jesus enters Jerusalem riding on a donkey
Jesus reveals that the Kingdom will be established at a definite time, as corroborated by world events. (Matthew 24:3-22 and Luke 21:24)
Jesus’ illustration of slaves receiving 5, 2, 1 talents (Mt 25)
Jesus’ illustration of sheep and goats (Mt 25:33)
Jesus goes with disciples to Gethsemane
Jesus is led to Annas, then to Caiaphas
Peter denies Jesus in the courtyard at Caiaphas’ house
Jesus is led to the Sanhedrin
Jesus is led back to Pilate
Simon of Cyrene is impressed into service to carry the torture stake for Jesus
Jesus is nailed to the torture stake
Jesus is placed in new tomb
Overpriests and pharisees request Pilate that the grave be made secure with guards
Sabbath ends - Mary Magdalene, Mary (mother of James) and Salome buy spices for greasing Jesus’ body
Maria Magdelena, Maria (mother of James) find first an empty grave, then two angels, then Jesus
Jesus appears to Cleopas and another disciple on the road to Emmaus
Jesus appears to the disciples in the locked room (Thomas is absent)
Jesus appears to the disciples in the locked room and convinces Thomas
Jesus appears at the Sea of Galilee and cause a miraculous catch
Jesus appears to the more than 500 disciples and commands them to preach
Jesus appears to the apostles and requests them to stay in Jerusalem and wait for what the Father has promised
Jesus walks from Jerusalem to Bethany together with the disciples
40 days after Nisan 16, Jesus returns to heaven
Pentecost, 50 days after Nisan 16, holy spirit was poured out
3000 baptized and anointed with holy spirit
End of 70 weeks of years (-455 to 36)
Caiaphas removed from office, committed suicide
Pilate removed from office and sent to Rome
Emperor Claudius begins a more permanent conquest of southern Britain
James (John’s brother) put in prison by Herod Agrippa I and executed
Peter is put in prison by Herod Agrippa I, but then released by an angel
Governing body decides that circumcision is not required
Paul chooses Timothy as a traveling companion
Roman Emperor Claudius requires all Jews to leave Rome
Jews in Corinth turn against Paul and bring him before judge Gallio
Paul explains that the Kingdom joint heirs will be resurrected to immortality and incorruption during Christ’s presence (1 Cor 15:51-54)
Paul travels to Jerusalem together with Tychicus for a relief mission
Paul arrested in Jeruzalem and brought before the Sanhedrin (Acts 21-23)
Philip lives in Caesarea together with his four daughters
Paul‘s defense before king Agrippa II (and Festus)
Paul first touched Italian soil at Rhegium on the ‘toe’ of Italy
Paul’s imprisonment in hired house in Rome
Fire that ravaged Rome, followed by persecution of Christians, at instigation of Nero
Zealots lead an assault on Roman guards in Jerusalem
Vespasian set about putting down the Jewish uprising
Nero’s unexpected death opens the way for Vespasian to become emperor
Vespasian returns to Rome and let his son Titus continue the campaign
Jerusalem and temple attacked by Romans (Titus)
Jerusalem and temple destroyed by Romans (Titus)
Masada, the last Jewish stronghold, falls to the Romans
Jesus, already ruling over his anointed followers, reveals that their final number will be 144,000 (Eph 5:32 and Col 1:13-20 and Rev 1:1 and 14:1-3)
John released from exile in Patmos
John dies (the last of the apostles)
Emperor Hadrian builds a wall from the Tyne River to the Solway Firth, marking the northern limit of the Roman Empire
Papyrus Rylands containing a few verses of the gospel of John written
Symmachus translates Hebrew Scriptures into Greek
Council of Carthage advocates infant baptism
Origen complets the Hexapla (a multiple version of the Hebrew Scriptures)
Diocletian decrees that Christian meeting places be razed and Scriptures burned
Theodosius I makes Christianity official religion of Roman Empire
Codex Sinaiticus (oldest complete copy of the New Testament in Greek) written
Roman Empire permanently divided into East and West
Council of Carthage: proposal to add 7 of the Apocryphal books and additions of Esther and Daniel to the Bible
Concilie van Tours - Local law that sermons had to be translated in the local language
Codex van Leningrad (oldest copy of the complete Bible in Hebrew)
Final break between Greek and Latin churches
Berengarius of Tours died (forerunner of the Reformation)
Petrus of Bruys died (forerunner of the Reformation)
Hendrik of Lausanne or of Cluny died (forerunner Reformation)
Paus Alexander III prohibits the Waldenzen to preach
Paus Innocentius III prohibits study of the Bible and Bibles in the language of the people
Council of Toulouse prohibits the use of vernacular Bibles
medieval Inquisition begins in Italy and France
First use of the Latin form ‘Jehovah’ in Pugio Fidei (this is not a Bibel)
Wycliffe’s Bible translation in English released
Erasmus produces Greek master text of Christian Scriptures
according to tradition, Luther nails 95 theses to church door
Complutensian Bible - Polyglot (first printed Greek Bible)
Tyndale completes English translation of Christian Scriptures
Robert Estienne publishes Bible editions in 1528-1557, introduces running heads and modern verse divisions
Tyndale produces English translation of Pentateuch
Calvin publishes Institutes of the Christian Religion
Matthew’s Bible printed in Antwerp, Belgium
King Henry VIII orders that all churches in England have a Bible

Council of Trent: Roman Catholic Church definitely confirms the acceptance of Tobit, Judith, additions to Esther, Wisdom, Ecclesiasticus, Baruch, 3 additions to Daniel, First Maccabees and Second Maccabees
Pope Paul IV forbids possession of Bible in common tongue
Geneva Bible (English) published in Geneva, Switzerland
Deuxaes Bible (first Dutch Translation containing the name Jehovah, in a drawing in the margin)
Psalms of Marnix of St.-Aldegonde (first Dutch translation using ‘Jehovah’ in the text)
Statenvertaling (first complete Dutch Bible translated from the source texts)
Treaty of Paris, British Empire dominant
Britain recognizes independence of 13 American colonies
Henry Grew publishes pamflet about immortal soul
George Storrs afrees with the reasoning of Henry Grew and shares it with others
George Storrs preaches about ‘are the wicked immortal?’
Parents of C.T. Russell move from Ireland to the US
Henry Grew, George Stetson and George Storrs expose the falseness of the immortality of the soul doctrine
Clay cylinders with cuneiform documents from King Nabonidus found in Ur (southern Iraq) that include a prayer for the unknown until then ‘Bel-sar-ussur, my eldest son’
Cuneiform tablets unearthed that referred to Belshazzar as having secretaries and household staff
Mother of C.T. Russel’s dies, after 2 of his brothers and a sisters died.
C.T. Russell begins careful study of Scriptures
C.T. Russell’s ‘wavering faith’ is reestablished after he hears a sermon by Adventist preacher Jonas Wendell
C.T. Russell and his associates understand that Christ’s presence would be invisible
C.T. Russell and a group from Pittsburgh and Allegheny (Pennsylvania, U.S.A.) begin systematic study of the Bible
Great Britain king of the south
C.T. Russell and his study group come to appreciate the ransom price
Russell withdraws all support from Herald of the Morning because of Barbour’s attitude toward the ransom.
Babylon the Great seen to be associated with Christendom
First issue of Zion’s Watch Tower and Herald of Christ’s Presence
Russell tours northeastern U.S. to form study groups
Zion’s Watch Tower Tract Society formed
Brothers in the US invited to meet together in a private home in Allegheny for the Memorial
Zion’s Watch Tower promotes the ministry
Watch Tower reaches China. Former Presbyterian missionary soon begins to witness to others there
Food for Thinking Christians reaches Liberia (Africa). Zion’s Watch Tower Tract Society is legally chartered in Pennsylvania. Officially recorded on December 15
Watch Tower publications are already being read by some people in North and South America, Europe, Africa and Asia
The Divine Plan of the Ages is published, the first volume of the series Millennial Dawn (later known as Studies in the Scriptures)
The Bible House is built on Arch Street in Allegheny (Pennsylvania) as headquarters for the Society
First gathering of Bible Students that they call a convention, in Allegheny, Pennsylvania (April 19-25)
Traveling overseers that in time came to be known as pilgrims are sent out to visit congregations
Name changes from Zion’s Watch Tower Tract Society to Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society
Zion’s Watch Tower points to 1914 as a year of great significance in the outworking of God’s ‘sacred secret’
First branch office is opened in London, England. Witnessing by Bible Students has been done in 28 countries and message they preach has reached 13 other lands
Russell-Eaton debates. Russell’s lectures appear regularly in newspapers
Intense house-to-house distribution of free tracts on Sundays (earlier much of the tract distribution was done on streets near churches
Second branch office established in Germany
Third branch office established in Australia
Sermons by C.T. Russell begin to appear regularly in newspapers. Within a decade they are being printed by about 2,000 papers
Watch Tower Society moves headquarters to Brooklyn (January 31)
name International Bible Students Association used
Appointed times of the nations ended
1260 days (Rev. 11:3). Anointed Christians dressed in sackcloth
Rutherford elected president of Watch Tower Society
Anglo-American alliance king of the south
8 brothers of the Society in prison
8 brothers of the Society released (officially from May 7, 1918 - March 25, 1919)
Watch Tower Society undertakes its own printing operations, in Brooklyn
Watch Tower studies begin in congregations
Cedar Point (Ohio) convention ‘Advertise, advertise, advertise the King and his Kingdom’
Watch Tower of March 1 shows that there are two distinct and opposing organizations - Jehovah’s and Satan’s
The Watch Tower explains that the Kingdom was born in 1914. The ‘sacred secret’ about the Kingdom must be publicized (Rev 12:1-5, 10, 17)
House-to-house preaching with books on Sunday is encouraged
Bible students are arrested in New Jersey (U.S.A.) for distributing literature as part of their house-to-house preaching. Within a decade, there are over 500 such arrests per year in U.S.A.
Christmas is understood to be a pagan celebration
Adoption of name Jehovah’s Witnesses (convention in Columbus, Ohio)
Sound cars and testimony cards used in field ministry
Portable phonographs are used by Witnesses
Great crowd clearly identified at convention in Washington D.C. (May 31, 1935)
The use of the cross is gradually discontinued
Signs ‘Religion Is a Snare and a Racket’
The election of elders is discontinued
J. F. Rutherford delivering the lecture ‘Face the Facts,’ at the Royal Albert Hall, in London, England. More than 10,000 jammed the auditorium, while millions more heard by radio
Throughout British Empire and British Commonwealth 23 nations ban Jehovah’s Witnesses or place prohibitions on literature
Active Witnesses pass the 100,000 mark, despite World War II
Archaeologists find evidence that supports the Biblical report about Mordecai
Association of nations foretold to rise again
The sanctity of blood is clarified
Soviet Union and its allies king of the north
Society’s board of directors is no longer selected by voters who qualify because of monetary donations
In Quebec, Canada, 1,700 cases involving the evangelizing work of Jehovah’s Witnesses are pending in the courts
New World Translation restores the divine name to its proper place
New World Translation of the Christian Greek Scriptures is released in English
The need for disfellowshipping is emphasized
An extensive program of training of Jehovah’s Witnesses in house-to-house preaching begins
The sanctity of marriage is clarified
Divine Will International Assembly, in New York, draws attendance of 253,922 from 123 lands. 7,136 baptized
First sessions of Kingdom Ministry School at South Lansing (New York), designed for congregation overseers and traveling overseers
For the first time, Bethel provided congregations with printed invitations for the Memorial
New World Translation released in English
Archaeologists find stone slab with Pilate’s name in Latin
New World Translation of the Christian Greek Scriptures is released in Dutch, French, German, Italian, Portuguese and Spanish
Waves of prolonged and savage persecution of Jehovah’s Witnesses sweep across Malawi and continue for years thereafter
Home Bible studies exceed a million
Governing Body is enlarged. Chairmanship begins to rotate among its members on an annual basis
Congregations of Jehovah’s Witnesses come under local supervision of body of elders, instead of one person
The use of tobacco is understood to be a serious wrongdoing
Branch offices come under supervision of a committee instead of one overseer
End of appointed times of nations in 1914 first announced
Instruction is given as to the types of employment that are acceptable for Christians
Home Bible studies exceed two million
Discovery of Caiaphas’ burial cave (high priest from 18 to 36 C.E.)
Bans lifted in Eastern Europe and Africa make it easier to reach 390,000,000 more people
Russia and its allies king of the north
Relief from persecution when the Soviet Union collapsed: ‘they will be given a little help’ (Dan. 11:34)
Program for the construction of Kingdom Halls in developing lands
The use of blood fractions is discussed in detail
U.S. Supreme Court upholds right to preach without permit
The Watchtower begins public and study editions
Evening for family worship established (instead of Congregation Book Study)
Congregation Bible Study becomes part of Theocratic Ministry School and the Service Meeting
Bible School for Single Brothers (instead of Ministerial Training School)
Bible School for Christian Couples begins
Special metropolitan witnessing with displays and literature begins
The seriousness of viewing abhorrent forms of pornography is clearly emphasized
School for Kingdom Evangelizers created (instead of Bible School for Christian Couples)
New World Translation released in Dutch
Public magazines to be issued three times a year
Meetings and field service worldwide via videoconference, phone or letters
Meetings in person (with mask) hybrid, field service still via phone or letters
Hour requirement of special, regular and auxiliary pioniers lowered to 100/90, 50, 30/15
Clarification on opportunity to side with Jehovah’s people even after Babylon the Great is destroyed
Adjusted approaches to the ministry (Love People)
No field service hours reporting anymore for publishers
Clarification with regard to wearing of a beard
Adjustment on treating a person who has been removed from the congregation and our standard of dress
Bethel service minimum age: 18, SKE minimum age: 21
Governing Body now consists of 11 members
Clarification on Revelation 17:13, 17 - give power and authority to UN.
Adjustment on our view on secular education
War between US and Israel versus Iran
Clarification on the medical use of one’s own blood
